title: Why Some Cities Banned Cars from City Centres
summary: A British narrator uses Oslo as a case study to explain how a city centre was emptied of cars, what went well, and what the rest of the world still gets wrong about copying it.
body: In 2019, the city of Oslo finished a project most engineers had thought impossible. It removed almost all private cars from its city centre. Streets that had carried thousands of vehicles a day were repaved for pedestrians, bicycles, and the occasional electric tram. Within two years, traffic deaths in the central zone had fallen to zero. Air quality had improved measurably, and pavement cafés had spread across districts that had been dominated by parked cars only a decade earlier. From a distance, this looks like a clean victory for the green movement. Look closer, and the picture is more complicated. The change did not happen because Oslo politicians woke up one morning and decided to be brave. It happened in small steps over more than a decade. First, parking spaces in the centre were slowly removed, year by year, so that drivers had increasingly fewer reasons to come in. Second, public transport was expanded — new tram lines, better bus frequency, and ferry routes that had been closed for years were reopened. Third, cycle lanes were built before private cars were removed, so that cyclists had a safe network ready when traffic patterns shifted. Fourth, deliveries, taxis, and disabled-access vehicles were exempted, and that exemption was published in detail so that no one could claim the city was punishing the elderly or small businesses. The fifth and most uncomfortable step was political honesty. Politicians told voters that the change would cost money, that some shops in the very first phase would lose foot traffic, and that two or three years of disruption were unavoidable. If they had pretended the transition would be painless, the project would almost certainly have collapsed during the difficult middle phase. Other cities have tried to copy Oslo and failed. The common mistake is to ban cars first and build alternatives afterwards. When a city does this, residents are furious, businesses lose customers overnight, and the next election usually reverses the policy. Madrid, Brussels, and several British towns have all made some version of this error. In each case the local press treated the failure as evidence that the policy itself was wrong, when really it was evidence that the sequence was wrong. None of this is to say that banning cars is the right answer everywhere. A small mountain town with no public transport is not Oslo. A spread-out American suburb is not Oslo. But for medium-sized European cities with reasonable public transport already in place, the lesson is clear. Quiet streets are not a luxury — they are a deliberate choice that takes a decade to make and a lot of patience to defend. The cities that try to shortcut the process tend to lose both the cars and the political will at the same time. Oslo's success is real, but it was earned, not declared.

annotations.meaningVi: được lát lại dành cho người đi bộ
annotations.exampleEn: The streets were repaved for pedestrians and bicycles.

annotations.meaningVi: số người chết do tai nạn giao thông
annotations.exampleEn: Traffic deaths fell to zero within two years.
Air quality has improved measurably since the policy began.
Pavement cafés have spread along the main square.
Parking was reduced year by year, not all at once.
New tram lines were opened to replace some car routes.
Cycle lanes were built before private cars were removed.
Taxis and disabled-access vehicles were exempted from the ban.
No one could claim the city was punishing the elderly.
The hardest step was political honesty about costs.

annotations.meaningVi: lượng khách bộ hành (đi qua cửa hàng)
annotations.exampleEn: Some shops would lose foot traffic in the first phase.
Most projects collapse during the difficult middle phase.
It wasn't the idea that failed — the sequence was wrong.

annotations.meaningVi: giao thông công cộng tương đối tốt
annotations.exampleEn: The lesson applies only to cities with reasonable public transport.

annotations.meaningVi: đi tắt, làm tắt quy trình
annotations.exampleEn: Cities that shortcut the process tend to lose both ways.

annotations.meaningVi: đạt được nhờ nỗ lực, không phải bằng tuyên bố
annotations.exampleEn: Oslo's success is real, but it was earned, not declared.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice and Past Perfect in Policy Narratives
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng kết hợp dạng bị động và quá khứ hoàn thành để mô tả chuỗi chính sách: 'parking spaces were slowly removed', 'cycle lanes were built before private cars were removed', 'ferry routes that had been closed for years were reopened'. Dạng bị động nhấn mạnh hành động/chính sách hơn là người thực hiện; quá khứ hoàn thành cho thấy điều gì xảy ra trước điều gì.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage combines passive voice and past perfect to narrate policy: 'parking spaces were slowly removed', 'cycle lanes were built before private cars were removed', 'ferry routes that had been closed for years were reopened'. Passive emphasises the action over the actor; past perfect clarifies the order of events.

grammarNotes.title: Third Conditional for Counterfactual Outcomes
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 để mô tả điều đã có thể xảy ra nhưng không: 'If they had pretended the transition would be painless, the project would almost certainly have collapsed'. Cấu trúc: 'if + had + V3, would have + V3'. Dùng để phân tích các điểm rẽ lịch sử và lập luận rằng kết quả thực tế không phải tự nhiên có.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses third conditional to describe outcomes that didn't happen: 'If they had pretended the transition would be painless, the project would almost certainly have collapsed.' Form: 'if + had + past participle, would have + past participle'. Use to analyse historical turning points and argue that the actual outcome was not inevitable.

translationVi: Năm 2019, thành phố Oslo hoàn thành một dự án mà hầu hết kỹ sư từng nghĩ là không thể. Họ đã loại bỏ gần như toàn bộ xe hơi cá nhân khỏi trung tâm thành phố. Những con phố từng chở hàng ngàn xe mỗi ngày đã được lát lại cho người đi bộ, xe đạp, và đôi khi là xe điện. Trong hai năm, số người chết vì giao thông ở khu trung tâm đã giảm xuống không. Chất lượng không khí cải thiện rõ rệt, và các quán cà phê vỉa hè lan ra khắp các khu vực mà chỉ một thập kỷ trước còn bị xe đỗ chiếm hết. Nhìn từ xa, đây giống như chiến thắng rõ ràng cho phong trào xanh. Nhìn gần hơn, bức tranh phức tạp hơn. Sự thay đổi không xảy ra vì các chính trị gia Oslo một buổi sáng tỉnh dậy và quyết định dũng cảm. Nó diễn ra trong các bước nhỏ qua hơn một thập kỷ. Thứ nhất, các chỗ đỗ xe ở trung tâm bị bỏ dần qua từng năm, để tài xế ngày càng ít lý do vào đó. Thứ hai, giao thông công cộng được mở rộng — các tuyến xe điện mới, tần suất xe buýt tốt hơn, và các tuyến phà đã bị đóng nhiều năm được mở lại. Thứ ba, làn xe đạp được xây trước khi xe hơi tư nhân bị loại bỏ, để người đi xe đạp có mạng lưới an toàn sẵn sàng khi luồng giao thông thay đổi. Thứ tư, xe giao hàng, taxi, và xe người khuyết tật được miễn trừ, và việc miễn trừ đó được công bố chi tiết để không ai có thể nói thành phố đang trừng phạt người già hoặc doanh nghiệp nhỏ. Bước thứ năm và khó chịu nhất là sự thẳng thắn chính trị. Các chính trị gia nói với cử tri rằng sự thay đổi sẽ tốn tiền, rằng một số cửa hàng trong giai đoạn đầu sẽ mất khách bộ hành, và rằng hai ba năm xáo trộn là không tránh khỏi. Nếu họ giả vờ quá trình chuyển đổi sẽ không đau đớn, dự án gần như chắc chắn đã sụp đổ trong giai đoạn giữa khó khăn. Các thành phố khác đã thử sao chép Oslo và thất bại. Sai lầm phổ biến là cấm xe trước rồi xây dựng phương án thay thế sau. Khi thành phố làm vậy, cư dân tức giận, doanh nghiệp mất khách trong một đêm, và cuộc bầu cử tiếp theo thường đảo ngược chính sách. Madrid, Brussels, và một số thị trấn Anh đều đã mắc phiên bản của lỗi này. Trong mỗi trường hợp, báo chí địa phương xem sự thất bại như bằng chứng rằng chính sách sai, trong khi thực ra nó là bằng chứng rằng trình tự sai. Tất cả điều này không có nghĩa là cấm xe là câu trả lời đúng ở mọi nơi. Một thị trấn miền núi nhỏ không có giao thông công cộng không phải là Oslo. Một vùng ngoại ô Mỹ trải rộng không phải là Oslo. Nhưng với các thành phố châu Âu cỡ trung đã có giao thông công cộng tương đối tốt, bài học rõ ràng. Phố yên tĩnh không phải là xa xỉ — chúng là một lựa chọn có chủ ý cần một thập kỷ để thực hiện và rất nhiều kiên nhẫn để bảo vệ. Các thành phố cố đi tắt thường mất cả xe lẫn ý chí chính trị cùng lúc. Thành công của Oslo là thật, nhưng nó được kiếm được, không phải được tuyên bố.

questions.prompt: When did Oslo finish its project to remove cars from the city centre?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'In 2019, the city of Oslo finished a project most engineers had thought impossible.'

questions.prompt: How many traffic deaths did the central zone have within two years of the project finishing?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'traffic deaths in the central zone had fallen to zero.'

questions.prompt: How long did the change actually take?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'It happened in small steps over more than a decade.'
questions.hint: The duration is described in years.

questions.prompt: What was Oslo's first concrete step?
options: Slowly removing parking spaces year by year
Building a new motorway around the city
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'parking spaces in the centre were slowly removed, year by year.'
questions.hint: The first step is gradual, not dramatic.

questions.prompt: What three things did Oslo do to expand public transport?
options: New tram lines, better bus frequency, and reopened ferry routes
Added high-speed rail to the suburbs
questions.explanation: The narrator lists 'new tram lines, better bus frequency, and ferry routes that had been closed for years were reopened.'

questions.prompt: Why were cycle lanes built before private cars were removed?
options: Because the cycle lanes were cheaper
So that cyclists had a safe network ready when traffic patterns shifted
Because car drivers refused to allow it later
Because they were paid for by a different department
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'cycle lanes were built before private cars were removed, so that cyclists had a safe network ready when traffic patterns shifted.'
questions.hint: The reasoning is about sequencing infrastructure before behaviour change.

questions.prompt: What vehicles were exempted from the ban?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'deliveries, taxis, and disabled-access vehicles were exempted.'
questions.hint: Three specific exempt categories are named.

questions.prompt: What was the most uncomfortable fifth step?
options: Political honesty — telling voters about costs, lost foot traffic, and unavoidable disruption
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The fifth and most uncomfortable step was political honesty.'
questions.hint: It was a communication step, not a physical one.

questions.prompt: What would have happened if politicians had pretended the transition would be painless?
options: The public would not have noticed
The project would almost certainly have collapsed during the difficult middle phase
The project would have finished sooner
Other cities would have copied it earlier
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'If they had pretended the transition would be painless, the project would almost certainly have collapsed.'
questions.hint: The narrator uses a hypothetical to make the point about honesty.

questions.prompt: What is the common mistake of cities that try to copy Oslo?
options: They spend too much money on cycle lanes
They ban cars first and build alternatives afterwards
They consult the public too often
They start in summer rather than winter
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The common mistake is to ban cars first and build alternatives afterwards.'
questions.hint: The mistake is about order of operations.

questions.prompt: Which cities does the narrator name as having made this error?
options: Madrid, Brussels, and several British towns
Los Angeles, Mexico City, and Rome
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Madrid, Brussels, and several British towns have all made some version of this error.'
questions.hint: Two named cities and a general region.

questions.prompt: Why does the narrator say this doesn't apply to all cities?
options: Because cars are essential in every city
Because the lesson only applies to medium-sized European cities with reasonable public transport already in place — not mountain towns or US suburbs
Because rich cities cannot afford it
Because the climate is too cold elsewhere
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'A small mountain town with no public transport is not Oslo. A spread-out American suburb is not Oslo.' but the lesson works for 'medium-sized European cities with reasonable public transport.'
questions.hint: The narrator names exceptions and the conditions where the lesson applies.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator's final sentence — 'Oslo's success is real, but it was earned, not declared' — mean?
options: Other cities should declare success quickly
Real change comes from sustained effort and patience, not from announcements
questions.explanation: The narrator contrasts earned (over a decade, with five steps) with declared (overnight bans without preparation).
questions.hint: Connect 'earned' to the patient sequence described throughout.

cloze.template: In 2019, the city of Oslo finished a project most engineers had thought impossible. It removed almost all private cars from its city centre. Within two years, traffic {{b1}} in the central zone had fallen to zero. Air quality had improved measurably. Pavement {{b2}} had spread across districts that had been dominated by parked cars only a decade earlier. The change happened in small steps over more than a decade. First, parking spaces were slowly removed, year by {{b3}}. Second, public transport was expanded. Third, {{b4}} lanes were built before private cars were removed, so that cyclists had a safe network ready. Fourth, deliveries, taxis, and disabled-access vehicles were {{b5}}. The fifth and most uncomfortable step was political {{b6}}. Politicians told voters that the change would cost money, that some shops would lose foot {{b7}}, and that disruption was unavoidable. Other cities have tried to copy Oslo and failed. The common mistake is to {{b8}} cars first and build alternatives afterwards. The local press treated the failure as evidence that the policy itself was wrong, when really it was evidence that the {{b9}} was wrong. The cities that try to {{b10}} the process tend to lose both the cars and the political will at the same time.

blanks.explanation: 'Traffic deaths' is the exact phrase the narrator uses — direct and journalistic.
'Pavement cafés' is the exact phrase — outdoor restaurants on widened sidewalks.
'Year by year' is the fixed idiom — incremental annual progress.
'Cycle lanes' is the standard British term — Oslo used the British/European usage in the passage.
'Exempted' is the precise legal/policy term — formally allowed to bypass a rule.
'Political honesty' is the exact phrase the narrator uses — direct truth-telling about costs.
'Foot traffic' is the retail term for pedestrians walking past a shop.
'Ban' is the exact verb — a formal prohibition.
'Sequence' is the exact noun used — the order of steps.
'Shortcut the process' is the exact phrase — to skip steps for speed.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Oslo succeeded by being honest with voters about pain and patience. Can a politician anywhere afford that kind of honesty today — and what conditions in a city or country make voters willing to accept short-term cost for long-term benefit?
